advaita.english: NOTE — Śaṅkarācārya's bhāṣya on the Gītā opens at 2.10; he provides no direct commentary on verse 1.1. The following rendering is therefore constructed from his introductory remarks (upodghāta) about the Gītā's purpose, not from verse-specific prose. Dharma-kṣetra and kuru-kṣetra are merely the locative frame within which Dhṛtarāṣṭra's anxious question is posed to Sañjaya — the question itself has no philosophical weight for Śaṅkara, who regards the first chapter as establishing only the occasion (nimitta) for Arjuna's grief, which will be diagnosed as avidyā (non-knowledge) in the second. The real inquiry has not yet begun.
key_terms_preserved: dharma-kṣetra (field where dharma takes root)
avidyā (non-knowledge underlying Dhṛtarāṣṭra's confusion)
nimitta (occasioning condition, not final cause)
advaita.divergence_note: BHĀṢYA ABSENT for this verse. Śaṅkara's commentary is attested only from 2.10 onward. This rendering is inferred from his upodghāta and his overall Gītā-framing; it should not be cited as direct Śaṅkara commentary on 1.1.

viśiṣṭādvaita.english: Rāmānuja reads this verse through the narrative arc his commentary spells out in full: Duryodhana has surveyed both armies and, weighing the Pāṇḍava force — protected by Bhīma — against his own — protected by Bhīṣma — inwardly grasped that his side, though formidable, is not equal to the task of defeating them. He reports this calculation of inadequacy (aparyāptatā) to his preceptor Droṇa and falls into despondency. Bhīṣma, perceiving that despondency, roars like a lion and sounds his conch to kindle joy in Duryodhana's heart — and the entire Kaurava camp erupts in drums and conchs. Then the Lord of all lords (sarveśvareśvara), seated with Pāṇḍunandana Arjuna on that great chariot which is the instrument of three-world conquest, blows the divine Pāñcajanya, shaking the three worlds — and the thunderous sound shatters the hearts of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's sons, who sense that the Kuru power has already perished. It is this accumulated narrative that Sañjaya now reports to Dhṛtarāṣṭra. The question 'what did they do?' is therefore answered not by a single act but by the entire unfolding from 1.1 to 1.19 that Rāmānuja treats as one continuous reply.
key_terms_preserved: sarveśvareśvara (Lord of all lords — Kṛṣṇa's ontological supremacy for Rāmānuja)
aparyāptatā (insufficiency — Duryodhana's inward assessment of his own force)
kainkarya (service — the charioteer's role as Bhagavān's freely chosen sārathya)
Pāñcajanya (Kṛṣṇa's conch — emblem of his sovereignty)
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Rāmānuja extends the reply to a single sweeping 1.1–1.19 narrative rather than glossing the verse in isolation. Kṛṣṇa's charioteer role is not a condescension but the supreme expression of his vātsalya (parental love) toward devotees. The conch-roar that shatters Kaurava hearts is read as a foretaste of Bhagavān's inevitable victory.

dvaita.english: NOTE — Madhvācārya's bhāṣya is not present in the supplied corpus for this verse. The following reflects Dvaita principles established in his Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya. Dhṛtarāṣṭra's question to Sañjaya opens under the pall of his own pāpa-karma (accumulated demerit): he is ontologically other than Hari, a jīva bound by his own nature, and his anxiety about 'my people' (māmakāḥ) reveals the tamas-driven delusion (moha) that blinds dependent souls to Hari's sovereignty. Kuru-kṣetra is the arena where Hari's will — not Duryodhana's strategy — will be executed. The battle's outcome is determined by Hari's sankalpā (sovereign will), not by troop arithmetic.
key_terms_preserved: māmakāḥ (my people — Dhṛtarāṣṭra's possessive delusion, ontologically impossible for Madhva since only Hari is svātantra, independent)
jīva (individual soul — eternally distinct from Brahman in Dvaita)
sankalpā (sovereign will — Hari's, not any human agent's)
tamas (inertia-quality — source of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's blindness)
dvaita.divergence_note: BHĀṢYA ABSENT for this verse. Rendering inferred from Madhva's Gītā-Tātparya-Nirṇaya and Dvaita principles. For Madhva, Dhṛtarāṣṭra's opening question is structurally a display of avidyā-rooted possessiveness (māmatā) — precisely the condition Dvaita diagnoses as the root of saṃsāra.

śuddhādvaita.english: Vallabhācārya binds 1.1 to 1.19 as a single undivided passage — the entire arc from Dhṛtarāṣṭra's anxious question through the Pāṇḍava conch-roar shattering the Kaurava host is one continuous moment in Kṛṣṇa's līlā (sportive play). The battlefield itself, the armies, the conch-blasts — all are the Lord's own expanding delight, not a tragedy requiring rescue. Dhṛtarāṣṭra asks from fear; the fear is also Kṛṣṇa's gift (prasāda), the first tremor of a world-drama the Lord has staged for his own rasa (aesthetic relish). Full commentary on this arc is transmitted through Viṭṭhaleśa Prabhu — Vallabha's son — whose sub-commentary carries the Puṣṭi-mārga reading forward.
key_terms_preserved: līlā (sportive divine play — all events are Kṛṣṇa's self-delight)
prasāda (grace-gift — including Dhṛtarāṣṭra's fear, which is itself Kṛṣṇa's doing)
rasa (aesthetic relish — the Puṣṭi-mārga's telos)
Puṣṭi-mārga (path of divine nourishment — Vallabha's school)
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Vallabha does not analyze the verse intellectually; he simply announces the scope (1.1–1.19) and defers the verse-by-verse exposition to Viṭṭhaleśa Prabhu's sub-commentary. His theological move is the framing itself: the whole first chapter is one unbroken experience of Kṛṣṇa's līlā-presence, not a problem requiring analysis.

bhakti.english: Śrīdhara Svāmī opens with the purpose of the entire text before glossing a single word: Bhagavān — whose feet all worlds worship, who is supremely compassionate (paramakāruṇika), who has descended for the welfare of all (saka-laloka-hitāvatāra) — will lift Arjuna from the ocean of grief and delusion (śoka-moha-sāgara) by means of the raft of teaching the secret of dharma-knowledge (dharma-jñāna-rahasya-upadeśa-plava). Arjuna's discrimination (viveka) has been overwhelmed by śoka (grief) and moha (delusion) arising from tattva-ajñāna (ignorance of reality), driving him toward abandoning his own dharma and adopting another's. Vyāsa encoded that divine teaching in seven hundred verses — writing mostly Kṛṣṇa's own words and composing a few connective verses himself, as authorized by the saying: 'The Gītā should be sung well — why multiply other scriptures? For it flows from the lotus-mouth of the Lotus-naveled One.' Dhṛtarāṣṭra now asks Sañjaya: on the dharma-ground (dharma-bhūmi) of Kuru-kṣetra, my sons and the Pāṇḍu-sons, eager for battle, having assembled — what did they do?
key_terms_preserved: paramakāruṇika (supremely compassionate — Bhagavān's descending motivation)
śoka-moha-sāgara (ocean of grief-and-delusion — what Arjuna is drowning in)
dharma-jñāna-rahasya-upadeśa-plava (raft of the secret teaching of dharma-knowledge — the Gītā's function for Śrīdhara)
viveka (discrimination — the faculty destroyed by śoka-moha)
dharma-bhūmi (ground of dharma — Śrīdhara's gloss on dharma-kṣetra)
bhakti.divergence_note: Śrīdhara anchors the opening in the descent-for-compassion theology (avatāra-karuṇā) before turning to narrative. His gloss on dharma-kṣetra as dharma-bhūmi (auspicious ground) is straightforward — no hidden double meaning. The verse is a stage-setter, and the stage's meaning is Bhagavān's compassion, not any philosophical puzzle about the field itself.

advaita-bhakti.english: Madhusūdana Sarasvatī opens his commentary not with the verse's words but with its place in the entire Gītā-architecture: the dialogue between Bhagavān and Arjuna functions as a vidyā (a praise-worthy knowledge-text), analogous to the Yājñavalkya-Janaka dialogues of the Upaniṣads, where a qualified student's crisis occasions the transmission of liberating insight. Arjuna's fall into śoka (grief) and moha (delusion) is analyzed with precision: it arises from the conflation of aham-eṣām (I am theirs) and mama-ete (they are mine) — an identity-superimposition rooted in ahaṃkāra (I-sense) binding him to fathers, teachers, sons, and friends. Even though Arjuna is already engaged in his own kṣatra-dharma (the warrior's duty of battle), this superimposition causes him to recoil from that duty and incline toward a renunciant's begging-life (bhikṣā-jīvana) — the dharma of another, explicitly forbidden for a kṣatriya. Regarding the battlefield itself, Madhusūdana goes further than any other commentator: dharma-kṣetra is the field where dharma is born from what was absent and grows from what was present — citing the Jābāla-śruti ('Kuru-kṣetra is the gods' sacrificial ground, the brahman-seat of all beings') and Śatapatha-śruti. His subtle reading of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's fear is unique: beyond the visible danger (dṛṣṭa-bhaya) of Bhīṣma and Arjuna, Dhṛtarāṣṭra fears an unseen danger (adṛṣṭa-bhaya) — the holy ground itself may purify even his wicked sons, and a change of heart would cost him the kingdom without any battle at all.
key_terms_preserved: vidyā (knowledge-text — the Gītā's genre for Madhusūdana, analogous to Upaniṣadic dialogues)
ahaṃkāra (I-sense — the root of śoka-moha for Madhusūdana)
aham-eṣām / mama-ete (I am theirs / they are mine — the specific superimposition causing Arjuna's collapse)
kṣatra-dharma (warrior-duty — from which Arjuna recoils)
adṛṣṭa-bhaya (unseen fear — Madhusūdana's unique reading of Dhṛtarāṣṭra's deeper anxiety about the holy ground's purifying power)
dharma-kṣetra as locus of dharma's arising and growth — cited against Jābāla- and Śatapatha-śruti
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Madhusūdana's adṛṣṭa-bhaya reading is unique in the panel: Dhṛtarāṣṭra is not merely worried about military odds — he fears that Kuru-kṣetra's sanctity may spiritually transform his sons, undoing his kingdom without battle. This synthesis of Advaita-ahaṃkāra analysis with Bhakti's vidyā-framing is characteristic: he preserves Śaṅkara's diagnostic category (superimposition / adhyāsa) while embedding the whole Gītā in the devotional vidyā tradition.

so_what_questions: Dhṛtarāṣṭra is blind yet asks Sañjaya what is happening on the battlefield — why does a man who has already chosen his side need a play-by-play from someone who can see clearly? What does that say about how we use information we already know will distress us?

He calls his sons 'māmakāḥ' (mine) and the Pāṇḍavas 'pāṇḍavāḥ' (theirs) in the same breath — is this just grammar, or is the entire war already encoded in that possessive pronoun? How much of our conflicts are really conflicts of 'mine vs. not-mine'?

The field is called dharma-kṣetra — a field of dharma — yet it hosts the most catastrophic battle in the tradition. Can a place named for righteousness be the site of mass destruction, or does that naming tell us something about what dharma actually demands?

Madhusūdana identifies a fear that Dhṛtarāṣṭra never voices — the dread that the holy ground itself might change his own children. What are the 'dharma-kṣetras' in modern life — the environments so charged with meaning that entering them transforms people against their families' wishes?

The question 'kim akurvata' (what did they do?) is asked after the armies are already assembled and eager to fight. At what point does asking 'what happened?' become a way of avoiding responsibility for setting it in motion?

Rāmānuja reads the conch-blast as Kṛṣṇa's sovereignty shaking three worlds — while a modern observer sees two armies psyching each other up before a fight. What does it mean to hold both readings simultaneously, and which one is more useful when you are the one about to enter the battle?

Every commentator treats chapter one as a prologue — the real text begins in chapter two. What does it mean that the tradition chose to preserve the hesitation, the grief, and the confusion rather than cutting to the answer? What is the function of recording a breakdown before the teaching?

advaita.english: Śaṅkara would say: notice that Dhṛtarāṣṭra's distress begins not with what is happening on the field but with the words 'māmakāḥ' and 'pāṇḍavāḥ' — mine and theirs. Every conflict you are currently carrying has the same grammatical structure: some version of 'what will happen to mine?' The Advaita practice here is not to become indifferent to outcomes but to trace the distress back to its root — the superimposition of a 'mine' onto what was never, in the deepest sense, possessable. When anxiety arrives today, ask not 'how do I fix this?' but 'who is the one who claims this as mine?' — and sit with that question until the answer loosens.
advaita.divergence_note: Inferred from Advaita principles; Śaṅkara's bhāṣya is absent for this verse.

viśiṣṭādvaita.english: Rāmānuja's commentary highlights Duryodhana's inward collapse — he surveys the field, calculates inadequacy, reports it to his preceptor, and falls despondent — all before a single arrow is shot. The Viśiṣṭādvaita application is this: when you have assessed your resources honestly and found them insufficient, that moment of clear-eyed acknowledgment is not defeat — it is the precondition for the Lord's intervention. Bhīṣma's conch-blast follows immediately upon Duryodhana's despondency. The practice is to report your honest assessment of your situation to someone wiser (ācārya, preceptor) and then remain open to the encouragement that follows — rather than either inflating your confidence with false bravado or collapsing entirely. Kainkarya (service to Bhagavān) does not require you to be adequate; it requires you to be honest.
viśiṣṭādvaita.divergence_note: Anchored in Rāmānuja's narrative of Duryodhana's despondency and Bhīṣma's response.

dvaita.english: Madhva's Dvaita would direct attention to Dhṛtarāṣṭra's use of 'māmakāḥ' — 'my people.' For Dvaita, the jīva (individual soul) is eternally dependent on Hari; no relationship is owned, only held in trust. The practical implication is sharp: when you feel that you must control an outcome because 'these are my people, my project, my legacy,' you are enacting Dhṛtarāṣṭra's error — claiming independence that no jīva actually possesses. The Dvaita discipline is to act with full effort while continuously returning ownership of outcomes to Hari, not as a passive shrug but as an active acknowledgment of ontological reality. Blind attachment — not effort — is what destroys Dhṛtarāṣṭra.
dvaita.divergence_note: Inferred from Dvaita principles; Madhva's bhāṣya is absent for this verse.

śuddhādvaita.english: Vallabha's Puṣṭi-mārga reads the whole field — armies, conchs, fear, and all — as Kṛṣṇa's expanding līlā (play). The application is not philosophical but experiential: whatever situation you are 'entering into battle' about today — a difficult conversation, a professional confrontation, a family reckoning — treat it not as a problem to be solved but as Kṛṣṇa's drama unfolding through you. You are both an actor in the scene and a witness to the Lord's delight in it. This does not mean passivity; it means bringing your full self to the moment while holding it lightly as the Lord's own story, not yours to control. The fear Dhṛtarāṣṭra feels is also prasāda — the Lord stirring the world into motion.
śuddhādvaita.divergence_note: Anchored in Vallabha's framing of 1.1–1.19 as a single līlā-arc.

bhakti.english: Śrīdhara names the Gītā's function before its first word: it is a raft (plava) for crossing the ocean of grief-and-delusion (śoka-moha-sāgara). The everyday application is diagnostic before it is prescriptive: when you are in the grip of a decision that feels impossible — when duty pulls one way and compassion pulls another — first name whether you are operating from viveka (clear discrimination) or from śoka-moha (grief-clouded confusion). Arjuna's confusion looks like ethical sensitivity; Śrīdhara sees it as his discriminative faculty being overwhelmed by attachment. The practice is to notice when compassion has curdled into paralysis, and to seek the company of teaching (upadeśa) that restores the capacity to act from clarity rather than from fear of loss.
bhakti.divergence_note: Anchored in Śrīdhara's avatāra-karuṇā framing and his gloss on śoka-moha as viveka-destruction.

advaita-bhakti.english: Madhusūdana's unique contribution is the adṛṣṭa-bhaya (unseen fear): Dhṛtarāṣṭra fears not only the Pāṇḍava army but the very ground — the possibility that the sacred field will transform his children's hearts and cost him the kingdom without any battle. The application for the working person is uncomfortably precise: how often do we avoid putting ourselves or those we love in genuinely transformative environments — a rigorous teacher, a demanding community, a practice that might change them — because we sense it could loosen our hold on them? Madhusūdana names this as a deeper fear than any visible threat. The Advaita-bhakti practice is to examine honestly which 'battlefields' you are keeping your people away from because you fear the ground itself more than the enemy.
advaita-bhakti.divergence_note: Anchored in Madhusūdana's unique adṛṣṭa-bhaya reading and his ahaṃkāra analysis.

self_assessment.bhashya_coverage: 4 of 6 schools have direct Sanskrit bhāṣya in the payload. Śaṅkara (advaita) and Madhva (dvaita) bhāṣyas are absent for 1.1 — both rendered from inferred principles with explicit disclosure. No undisclosed inference.
self_assessment.fabrication_risk: Low for Rāmānuja, Śrīdhara, Madhusūdana, Vallabha — all four renderings are anchored in supplied Sanskrit prose. Moderate for Śaṅkara and Madhva — both are clearly flagged as inferred.
self_assessment.sridhara_artifact_check: Śrīdhara payload is clean Sanskrit prose with no HTML or JS artifacts. Rendered directly from supplied bhāṣya.
self_assessment.constraint_compliance: No English suffixes on Sanskrit roots. Sanskrit terms appear in romanized Sanskrit with parenthetical glosses. doctrinal_renderings is a dict keyed by school name. Bhāṣya absences flagged in divergence_note and in english text.
self_assessment.divergence_authenticity: Meaningful doctrinal divergence present: Śaṅkara treats 1.1 as philosophically inert (occasion-setting); Rāmānuja reads a sweeping devotional narrative; Madhva emphasizes jīva-dependence and possessive delusion; Vallabha refuses analysis in favor of līlā-framing; Śrīdhara foregrounds compassionate descent and grief-diagnosis; Madhusūdana adds the unique adṛṣṭa-bhaya reading and ahaṃkāra analysis not present in other commentators.
